industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Glenn T. Seaborg — the only element named after a living person at the time of naming (1994). Produced at Berkeley by bombarding californium-249 with oxygen-18 ions. Gas-phase chemistry at PSI shows Sg forms volatile SgO2Cl2 analogous to WO2Cl2, confirming its position as a heavier tungsten congener.
Longest-lived known isotope: 271Sg, t½ = 2.4 minutes. First synthesized: 1974, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (Ghiorso, Nitschke, Morrissey, Seaborg group).

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
companies.use: Discovered element 106 in 1974 using the SuperHILAC accelerator. The naming controversy (Seaborg objected to naming an element after a living person initially) was resolved when IUPAC approved seaborgium in 1997. Berkeley produced all early Sg samples used for nuclear property measurements.

companies.name: Paul Scherrer Institute (PSI)
companies.use: Performed the first gas-phase chemistry of seaborgium using the online isothermal gas chromatography system, identifying volatile seaborgium oxychloride SgO2Cl2 — confirming Sg as a chemical homolog of tungsten in Group 6.

companies.name: GSI Helmholtz Centre
companies.use: Produced seaborgium using lead targets and confirmed nuclear decay properties. GSI's SHIP separator enabled high-sensitivity detection of individual Sg atoms, contributing to the nuclear database used for chemical experiments.
